research_brief: You're exploring product ideas in field-service scheduling and dispatch. Priya agreed to give you ten minutes between jobs.

persona.name: Priya Raman
persona.company_context: Desert Air Mechanical, a 60-person HVAC and plumbing company in Phoenix
persona.visible_profile: Priya has run operations at Desert Air Mechanical for five years. The company does residential and light-commercial HVAC and plumbing across the Phoenix metro with about 22 field techs. She agreed to a short research chat between morning dispatch and a vendor call.
persona.voice_notes: Brisk and direct, says 'look' and 'at the end of the day', answers in complete sentences, warms up noticeably when telling logistics war stories, mildly impatient with vague questions.

facts.content: Dispatch runs on a whiteboard that gets photographed into a Slack channel at 6:30 every morning; two dispatchers then reconcile changes by phone all day.
facts.unlock: any open question about how scheduling or dispatch works today
facts.why_it_matters: Confirms the core process is manual and fragile.

facts.content: Double-bookings or missed appointment windows happen four to six times a week, worse during the summer AC surge.
facts.unlock: open question about how often things go wrong
facts.why_it_matters: Establishes the pain is frequent, not occasional.

facts.content: Last Tuesday a tech drove 40 minutes to a job that had been cancelled the night before; the cancellation existed only in a voicemail nobody logged.
facts.unlock: question asking for a recent specific failure or example
facts.why_it_matters: A concrete, recent, costly incident; the kind of story that proves the pain is live.

facts.content: In the first week of June they lost a $9,400 annual commercial maintenance contract after two techs showed up to the same job while another client waited three hours; that client left a one-star review naming the company.
facts.unlock: follow-up probing the consequences or cost of a failure already mentioned
facts.why_it_matters: Quantified business damage tied directly to the problem.

facts.content: Paid $1,150 in dispatcher overtime last month purely on untangling scheduling conflicts, and pays a part-time evening coordinator $800 a month who mostly re-confirms appointments by phone.
facts.unlock: direct question about what the current process costs in money or hours; deflects once before answering
facts.why_it_matters: Roughly $2,000 a month of existing spend is the budget a solution would displace.

facts.content: Trialed a major field-service software suite 14 months ago and ripped it out after six weeks because techs over 45 wouldn't use the mobile app and the owner refused to mandate it; current software spend beyond QuickBooks and Slack is zero.
facts.unlock: question about tools tried before and why they failed
facts.why_it_matters: There is a failed-adoption scar; any new solution inherits this skepticism.

facts.content: Priya can sign off on ops software up to $600 a month on her own authority; anything above that goes to the owner, who has been tech-skeptical since the failed trial.
facts.unlock: direct question about who decides on purchases or how the last buying decision happened; deflects once before answering
facts.why_it_matters: She is a real buyer with a real ceiling; pricing above $600/mo changes who the customer is.

facts.content: Fixing dispatch is her number one written objective for Q3 and her bonus is tied to reducing missed appointments.
facts.unlock: question about her goals, priorities this quarter, or what she is measured on
facts.why_it_matters: Top-priority pain with personal incentive attached; the strongest possible buying signal.

facts.content: Anything that requires field techs to learn a new app is dead on arrival; the field side has to work over SMS or plain phone calls, and the office side must export to QuickBooks.
facts.unlock: a third-level probe on why the last tool failed, or a direct question about hard requirements and constraints
facts.why_it_matters: Kills most naive 'just build an app for the techs' ideas; the wedge must be SMS-first.

facts.content: She is embarrassed that a photographed whiteboard is the system of record; she hides it when clients visit the office and once told a vendor they use 'proprietary software'.
facts.unlock: a probe about how she feels about the current setup after some rapport
facts.why_it_matters: Emotional charge means the problem sells itself internally once a credible fix exists.
